Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11042-025-20604-9




Two stream CNN for muzzle print recognition using gabor
filters

Newlin Shebiah Russel1            · Arivazhagan Selvaraj1

Received: 5 October 2023 / Revised: 7 August 2024 / Accepted: 2 January 2025 /
Published online: 20 January 2025
© The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature 2025


Abstract
Muzzle print recognition, an essential aspect of biometric identification for animals, has
gained significant attention in recent years. This study introduces a novel method for
enhancing the precision and robustness of muzzle print identification through the utiliza-
tion of a Two-Stream Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) integrated with Gabor Filters.
The proposed Two-Stream CNN architecture comprises of two distinct streams, the Spatial
Stream, which processes raw muzzle images, and the Texture Stream, dedicated for extract-
ing texture features through Gabor Filters. By integrating feature maps from both streams,
the model effectively utilizes the synergistic advantages of spatial and texture information,
thereby augmenting the discriminative capabilities of the recognition system. The Gabor
Filters applied in the Texture Stream enable the extraction of intricate texture details, mak-
ing the model highly sensitive to variations in muzzle patterns. By conducting training and
fine-tuning procedures on animal muzzle prints in the Beef Cattle Muzzle/Nose print data-
base for individual identification, a recognition accuracy of 98.99% was achieved. Addi-
tional performance metrics include a recall of 98.36%, precision of 98.39%, specificity of
100% and F1-Score of 98.18%. The ability of the proposed model to accurately differenti-
ate intricate variations in muzzle prints presents significant prospects for diverse fields of
application, such as wildlife preservation, animal monitoring, and security measures.

Keywords Muzzle Print Recognition · Two-Stream CNN · Gabor Filters · Biometric
Identification · Animal Conservation


1 Introduction

In today’s dynamic farming landscape, the success of any agricultural venture depends
on the art of effective livestock management. The trinity of efficiency, affordability, and
scalability has emerged as the cornerstone of livestock management solutions within the


* Newlin Shebiah Russel
  newlinshebiah@mepcoeng.ac.in
1
    Centre for Image Processing and Pattern Recognition, Department of Electronics
    and Communication Engineering, Mepco Schlenk Engineering College, Sivakasi, Tamilnadu,
    India


                                                                                         Vol.:(0123456789)
34286                                         Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


realm of modern farmsteads. The surge in this domain is attributed to its wide array of
applications, such as cattle identification, monitoring the natural habitat of livestock, track-
ing animal herds, analyzing the health trajectory of cattle, and optimizing food production
management.
   The conventional methods employed for herd management are characterized by a high
demand for manual work and intrusive procedures that carry the risk of inducing discom-
fort and illness in livestock. Various methods have been employed to monitor and distin-
guish individual animals within herds, encompassing ear tagging, ear tattooing, hot iron-
ing, freeze branding, ID collars, microchipping, and visual markers like paint [1]. Both
metallic and plastic ear tags have been found to potentially cause harm [2] and significant
discomfort to livestock [3]. The process of ear tattooing necessitates the careful manipula-
tion and control of livestock, while its visibility is limited to close proximity [4]. Research
has shown that both hot iron and freeze branding methods have been found to induce suf-
fering in cattle [5]. Additionally, the use of collar straps still necessitates manual handling
of livestock and presents potential risks such as collar loss or entanglement [6]. Achiev-
ing minimum harm during horse monitoring necessitated meticulous design and strategic
placement of VHF/GPS collars [7]. It is reasonable to anticipate that comparable chal-
lenges would arise in the context of cattle monitoring. The utilization of electronic identity
tags and collars, exemplified by the National Livestock identity Tags, necessitates meticu-
lous record-keeping and management practices, which inevitably result in a certain per-
centage of tag failures [8]. Additional electronic methodologies, such as microchipping,
necessitate the administration of subcutaneous injections [9] or the insertion of alternative
electronic transponders or sensors into anatomical regions such as the scutulum [10, 11].
   The utilization of animal biometrics in cattle recognition systems introduces effective
approaches for registering livestock animals based on their distinct biometric attributes.
This innovative system not only enhances the process of livestock registration but also
provides superior techniques for monitoring and ensuring traceability of individual cattle
within farm environments [2, 4]. These characteristics include a range of attributes per-
taining to the animal’s physical attributes, including its muzzle pattern, face features, horn
structure, coat markings, as well as body size and proportions. Biometric muzzle prints
relate to a distinct pattern or image obtained from the muzzle region, which corresponds
to the anterior portion of an animal’s face. These prints are commonly employed for the
purpose of identification. The muzzle pattern, which exhibits a correlation with human fin-
gerprints, has been recognized as a potential biometric identifier for cattle for identification
purposes. This methodology involves the utilization of specific technological tools, such
as cameras or sensors, to collect and analyze the distinctive patterns exhibited by animal
muzzles.
   A substantial amount of prior research has been dedicated to the examination of com-
puter-assisted techniques for the identification of cattle based on their muzzle prints. Vari-
ous models have been employed for this task, such as deep learning [12], support vector
machines [13–15], group sparse representation [16], fuzzy-k-nearest neighbor [17], Fisher
locality projections [13], decision trees [18], AdaBoost classifiers [19], SIFT matching,
bag-of-visual words [20], and classifier fusion for the purpose of identifying individual
cattle.
   The deep learning approach is currently gaining prominence in the field of computer
vision as a means of detecting and representing the phenotypic appearances and visual
biometric features of species and individual animals. In recent years, there has been an
increased focus on deep learning as one of the most effective methods for extracting and
representing features of various species or individual animals [21–23]. Deep learning-based
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                                   34287


learning frameworks are employed to acquire effective learning capacity for feature repre-
sentations. These frameworks are utilized to extract sets of biometric features of species
for the purpose of representation and identification [24, 25]. The deep learning architecture
encompasses various layers that are employed to effectively model and depict intricate dif-
ferences in data for the purpose of animal recognition [26–29].
   The muzzle point image pattern consists of rich and dense texture features. These dis-
tinctive ridges and furrows emerge due to differences in skin texture, the distribution of fur,
and other inherent physical traits. The proposed cattle recognition system recognizes the
cattle based on texture feature of muzzle point image. The system uses deep learning based
recognition frameworks.
   The contributions of this work encompass several key aspects:

1. This research introduces a novel Two-Stream Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
   architecture that effectively combines spatial and texture-based features for muzzle print
   recognition. This innovative design enhances the model’s ability to capture and discrimi-
   nate intricate patterns on animal muzzles.
2. By incorporating Gabor Filters into the Texture Stream of the CNN, a valuable texture
   analysis technique that significantly improves the model’s sensitivity to subtle variations
   in muzzle prints, leading to more accurate recognition results.
3. By multiplying feature maps, the network can amplify features that are deemed dis-
   criminative for a particular task. This improves the network’s ability to make accurate
   predictions or classifications based on these enhanced features.



2 Literature survey

Kaur et al. [30] conducted a study focusing on livestock identification using the Shi-Tomasi
corner detector. This research involves feature extraction using prominent feature descrip-
tors, Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) and Speed up Robust Features (SURF). To
expedite computational efficiency, they employed a linear dimensionality reduction tech-
nique. In the realm of classification, the study explored three distinct strategies: Multi-layer
Perceptron (MLP), Random Forest, and Decision Tree classifiers. Hadad et al. [31] intro-
duced two distinct classification models for bovines, leveraging Artificial Neural Network
(ANN) and K-Nearest Neighbour Classifier (KNN) techniques. To extract muzzle features,
the ANN model utilizes Segmentation-based Fractal Texture Analysis (SFTA). On the
other hand, the K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) model consists of two phases: the Expecta-
tion Maximization (EM) picture segmentation phase and the classification phase.
   Edwin and George [32] used Eigen Muzzle as a dimensionality reduction technique.
The projected feature space is constructed using fuzzy membership functions. Bello et al.
[33] used of stacked denoising auto-encoders and deep belief networks for enhancing deep
learning in cow nose image texture extraction. Experimental results across various identifi-
cation scenarios indicate that deep belief networks outperform other approaches, achieving
an accuracy rate of approximately 98.99%. Girish et al. [34] proposed a comprehensive
approach for Muzzle Detection, alignment, feature extraction and classification.
   Kumar et al. [35] employs texture feature descriptors (SURF and LBP) at different
Gaussian smoothing levels, achieving impressive 93.87% identification accuracy on a 500-
cow image database. Awad et al. [36] utilizes Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT)
algorithm to identify distinctive points for image matching. To further improve resilience,
34288                                       Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


the Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) algorithm is integrated with the SIFT out-
put to eliminate outlier points, enhancing overall robustness. Shojaeipour et al. [37] used
YOLOv3-ResNet50 for detecting and extracting cattle muzzle regions in images. Subse-
quently, this algorithm employs deep transfer learning techniques for biometric identifi-
cation. Li et al. [38] compiled and shared a high-quality dataset containing 4923 muzzle
images from 268 US feedlot finishing cattle, with each animal having more than 12 images
captured using a mirrorless digital camera, and evaluated and compare the effectiveness of
various deep learning models in accurately identifying individual beef calves. The com-
prehensive assessment involved 59 deep learning image classification models, achieving
the highest accuracy of 98.7% in identifying the 268 animals. Awad and Hassaballah [39]
investigated the effectiveness of the Bag-of-Visual-Words (BoVW) method for cattle iden-
tification using local invariant features extracted from a database of muzzle print images.
The BoVW model employs two local invariant feature detectors, namely speeded-up
robust features (SURF) and maximally stable extremal regions (MSER), to extract relevant
features.
    Jayakumar et al. [40] begins with a training procedure to construct a model for local-
izing cow faces, utilizing a Haar feature-based cascade classifier. Subsequently, the water-
shed algorithm is employed to perform segmentation on a specific area of interest (ROI)
– the cattle’s muzzle region – within the identified face region. Enhancement techniques
are applied to the muzzle ROI to emphasize ridge lines. The final step involves using two
methods to derive a primary feature descriptor, which combines a bag of histograms of
oriented gradients (BoHoG) and a histogram of local binary patterns (LBP). Alaa Tharwat
[41] relies on the Local Binary Pattern (LBP) technique to extract local invariant features
from muzzle print images. For livestock identification, a range of classifiers, including
Nearest Neighbor, Naive Bayes, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and K-Nearest Neighbor
(KNN) is employed.
    Ketmaneechairat et al. [42] used the Scale Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT) for
detecting salient points and the Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) method for remov-
ing outlier points, thereby bolstering image matching robustness. Kumar & Singh [43]
introduced a hybrid feature extraction methodology for the automated identification and
categorization of cow breeds. The proposed strategy uses extraction of a set of prominent
features, and the application of various classification models, including K-nearest neighbor
(K-NN), Fuzzy-KNN, Decision Tree (DT), Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), Probabilistic
Neural Network (PNN), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and Naive Bayes for classification.
Sian et al. [44] combines textural information derived from the Weber Local Descriptor
(WLD) and Local Binary Pattern (LBP) to represent individual cattle. The WLD algorithm
was enhanced in several ways to improve its performance. Ultimately, a Support Vector
Machine (SVM) was employed to differentiate cattle based on the fusion of these features.
The approach presented in this study achieved an impressive identification accuracy of
96.5%.



3 Proposed methodology

The fundamental task of cattle muzzle print recognition lies in the extraction of attributes
such as texture, contour, and ridge characteristics. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
have become increasingly popular as a feature learning tool due to their exceptional per-
formance, as evidenced by a review of the existing literature. This accomplishment with
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                                  34289


CNNs is a compelling reason to use them for the extraction of distinguishing features in
cattle muzzle prints. A novel deep learning framework that is specifically designed for the
recognition of cattle muzzle prints is illustrated in Fig. 1. The integration of two paral-
lel sources within this proposed architecture enables the fusion of feature maps that are
derived from both spatial and texture information.
    In the field of image processing, Gabor filters [45] are frequently employed for the pur-
pose of texture analysis and feature extraction. These filters can be incorporated into a
CNN architecture in order to accomplish the Muzzle Print Recognition System. Gabor fil-
ters are applied to the input images to extract texture features, as these bandpass filters are
adept at capturing texture information across various scales and orientations. A two-stream
CNN architecture with separate branches is proposed: the Spatial Stream processes the raw
input images through a series of convolutional layers to learn spatial features, followed by
pooling layers to downsample the feature maps and reduce spatial dimensions, while the
Texture Stream processes the Gabor-filtered images. The outputs from both streams are
then fused by multiplication.
    The first stream in our proposed network is designed to process muzzle images that are
128 × 128 pixels in size and have three color channels (RGB) as input. To extract intricate
features fixed convolutional kernel sizes of 3 × 3 and max-pooling layers with 2 × 2 kernel
sizes are used.
    In more detail, the Conv_1 layer and Conv_G_1 takes input images of size 128 × 128 × 3
and employs 46, 3 × 3 kernels, resulting in a feature map of size 128 × 128 × 46. The
computation involves 1,288 learnable parameters, which are calculated as follows:
(3 × 3 × 3 × 46) + 46. Conv_G_1 employs 46 Gabor filters with varying scales and orienta-
tions. Gabor filters are known for their optimal localization properties in both spatial and
frequency domains, enabling them to extract a maximal amount of information from local
regions within the image. Additionally, Gabor features exhibit invariance to translation,




Fig. 1  Two Stream Architecture for Muzzle Print Recognition
34290                                               Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


rotation, and scale transformations. On the other hand, Conv_C_1 utilizes learnable filters
whose weights adapt during the training process based on the provided training data. These
filters are versatile and flexible, allowing the network to learn and adjust its parameters to
the specific features and patterns present in the cattle muzzle images encountered during
training. This adaptability enhances the network’s ability to discriminate between different
muzzle prints effectively.
    A Gabor filter is a spatial linear filter that employs a Gaussian kernel that is modulated
by a sinusoidal plane wave. This filter is designed to capture specific patterns and textures
within an image. One distinctive feature of the Gabor filter is its ability to represent ori-
entation information, and to achieve this, it utilizes both real and imaginary components.
These real and imaginary components can be combined into a single complex number, as
described by Eq. 1 Fig. 2.
                                              ( �2
                                                  x + 𝛾 2 y�2
                                                              )
                                                                       x�
                  g(x, y;𝜆, 𝜃, 𝜓, 𝜎, 𝛾) = 𝑒𝑥𝑝 −         2
                                                               exp(i(2𝜋 + 𝜓)               (1)
                                                     2𝜎                𝜆

x and y are the spatial coordinates of the image and x′ and y′ are the coordinates in the
rotated frame, defined as: x� = xcos𝜃 + ysin𝜃 and y� = −xsin𝜃 + ycos𝜃.
    In the Eq. (1), parameters play key roles in defining the characteristics of the Gabor
filter is as follows:

– λ represents the wavelength of the sinusoidal component, determining the spacing of
  the filter’s wave-like pattern.
– Ө denotes the orientation of the Gabor filter, indicating the direction of the wave-like
  pattern in the filter.
– Ψ represents the phase offset, indicating the starting phase of the sinusoidal wave.
– σ is the standard deviation of the Gaussian envelope, determining the spread or width
  of the Gaussian component.
– ɣ is the spatial aspect ratio, which controls the elongation of the filter in a particular
  direction.

   For experimentation purposes, a total of 46 Gabor filters were created. These filters cov-
ered a range of orientations from −45 degrees to + 45 degrees, with increments of 2 degrees
between each orientation. This range of orientations allows the filters to capture various




Fig. 2  Gabor Filters used as Customized Filters for Feature Extraction
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                                    34291


directional information present in the input images, helping to extract important features
related to texture, contour, and ridge patterns.
    In the network architecture, the Conv_2 and Conv_G_2 layers are essential for enhanc-
ing and expanding feature representations. The Conv_2 layer applies 64 convolutional filters
to the feature maps obtained from the Conv_1 layer in the spatial stream, thereby capturing
more complex and abstract features with increased depth. This expansion allows the network
to detect intricate patterns and details not fully captured by Conv_1. Similarly, the Conv_G_2
layer processes the Gabor-filtered images from Conv_G_1 in the texture stream, using 64 fil-
ters to extract detailed texture-specific features. Following this, the Conv_3 layer builds upon
the features extracted by Conv_2 by applying 128 convolutional filters. This increase in filter
depth enables the Conv_3 layer to capture even more complex and nuanced patterns, enhanc-
ing the network’s ability to discriminate between different classes. Together, these layers dem-
onstrate a progressive deepening of feature extraction, where each successive layer increases
in filter depth to capture increasingly sophisticated and detailed representations of the input
data.
    After the feature extraction process, the network may utilize multiplication operations to
enhance and integrate features effectively. Specifically, element-wise multiplication of feature
maps can be employed to combine features from different sources or layers, allowing the net-
work to refine and unify its feature representations. In some cases, this multiplication is inte-
gral to attention mechanisms, where it is used to weigh and prioritize different feature maps or
layers. By focusing on more relevant features, attention mechanisms leverage these multiplica-
tion operations to improve the network’s performance in distinguishing important patterns and
details, thereby enhancing the overall feature extraction and representation process.
    After the multiplication operations, the network proceeds with a convolutional layer of
depth 256. This layer further refines and consolidates the features by applying a larger number
of filters, allowing for the capture of even more complex patterns and relationships within the
data. Following this, the network includes one or more fully connected layers, which integrate
the high-level features extracted by the convolutional layers. The final layer is a softmax clas-
sification layer, which transforms the network’s output into probability distributions over the
possible classes, enabling the classification of the input data based on the learned features.
This sequence of operations—convolution, feature integration, and classification—ensures a
comprehensive approach to feature extraction and decision-making.
    The proposed hybrid CNN architecture integrates both handcrafted features and deep fea-
tures obtained from learnable filters to effectively classify input images into their respective
categories. This approach capitalizes on the feature learning capabilities for which CNNs are
renowned, and it enhances this process by incorporating manually crafted features. In this
hybrid architecture, two parallel CNNs are trained from scratch. These CNNs are designed
with a specific number of convolutional and pooling layers engineered to yield feature
maps of the same size for subsequent fusion. Following this, fully connected layers lead to
the final softmax layer, which produces probability scores for each class, facilitating image
classification.


4 Results and discussion

This section, the empirical findings of the proposed two-stream CNN model’s performance
in cattle muzzle print recognition is elaborated.
34292                                            Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


4.1 Beef cattle muzzle/noseprint database for individual identification

Beef Cattle Muzzle/Noseprint database for individual identification dataset comprises
images of beef cattle muzzles and noseprints. The dataset was gathered between March
and July 2021 in the Midwest US, encompassing a total of 4,923 muzzle images captured
from 268 feedyard yearlings. Additionally, 4,531 raw images were collected from 400 US
mixed-breed cattle, including Angus, Angus × Hereford, and Continental × British cross-
breeds, with a specific focus on the muzzle area. Individual identification of the cattle was
ensured by recording their ear tag information. These images were captured in natural
outdoor feedlot conditions, resulting in variations in angles and lighting. To enhance the
dataset’s utility for classification purposes, efforts were made to eliminate unnecessary ana-
tomical parts, such as the face, eyes, and body, by rotating the cattle’s face area to align
the muzzle horizontally. Subsequently, the muzzle area was manually cropped to isolate
it. Images that were excessively blurry, incomplete, or obscured by feed were excluded to
maintain dataset quality. Despite potential similarities in colors and textures among indi-
viduals, the distinct patterns of beads, grooves, and ridges provided valuable visual distinc-
tions for individual identification. Figure 3 shows some sample images from Beef Cattle
Muzzle/Noseprint database.

4.2 Visualizing feature maps produced by spatial and texture streams

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are specifically designed for processing image
data, and they provide us with valuable feature maps generated by their filters. Visualizing
these feature maps and the filters themselves offers a profound insight into the characteris-
tics that these filters have learned. From Fig. 4, it is observed that the activations produced




Fig. 3  Sample images from Beef Cattle Muzzle/Noseprint database
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                                    34293


by the initial convolution layers in two parallel streams. In Fig. 4 (a i), the activations stem-
ming from the Convolution Layer 1 of the spatial stream is visualized. These activations
reveal that this layer has learned to identify smooth regions and emphasize the distinguish-
ing features exhibited by the muzzle print. In Fig. 4 (a ii), it is observed the input muzzle
image alongside the feature map, highlighting the highest activations among the 46 feature
maps. In Fig. 4(b), we encounter the activations originating from the Convolution Layer 1
of the texture stream, which utilizes Gabor filters. The filtered output from these custom-
ized filters, also known as Gabor Masks, clearly exposes the patterns, making evident the
distinct changes such as edges and spots. Figure 4 (b ii) reveals the input muzzle image in
conjunction with the feature map, emphasizing the most prominent activations among the
46 feature maps within the texture stream.
    Figure 5 displays the activations originating from Convolution Layer 2 within both the
spatial and texture streams. As visualized in Fig. 5, these learnable filters have the capa-
bility to capture both coarse information about the overall characteristics of the muzzle
print and distinct features of the muzzle, such as unique patterns or marks. The learnable
filters after Gabor feature maps are successful in discerning and emphasizing these impor-
tant attributes, making them more prominent in the generated feature maps as visualized in
Fig. 5 (b ii). This demonstrates the adaptability and effectiveness of the neural network in
learning and representing features during the training process. Figure 5 (b i) and Fig. 5 (b
ii) depict activation maps with the highest values to ensure good visualization.
    As we progress through the layers of the neural network, including subsequent lay-
ers beyond Convolution Layer 2, the network’s learning process continues to unfold with
increasing complexity and abstraction. Convolution Layer 2 contains its own set of filters,
also known as learnable filters, which have undergone training to specialize in recogniz-
ing more intricate patterns and finer details within the muzzle print. These filters may be
designed to identify specific characteristics such as fur texture, subtle markings, or unique
contours. Figure 6 (a i) and Fig. 6 (a ii) exhibit the activations stemming from the third
convolutional layers, while their maximum values are presented in Fig. 6 (b i) and Fig. 6 (b
ii) correspondingly.




Fig. 4  Activations from Ist Layer
34294                                       Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301




Fig. 5  Activations from 2nd Layer



   When the feature maps from two different streams in a neural network, such as in a
multi-stream architecture, are multiplied together, it results in a form of feature fusion or
interaction between the information encoded in those feature maps. Multiplying feature
maps effectively captures the element-wise interaction or correlation between the features
extracted by the two streams. If two feature maps have high activations at the same spatial
locations, their product will have a higher value, highlighting regions where both streams
agree on important features. Multiplication blends information from both streams, creating
a fused representation that incorporates aspects from each stream. This fusion can be use-
ful for capturing complementary information or enhancing the saliency of shared features.
Multiplication can act as a form of attention mechanism. If one stream has learned to focus
on certain features or regions while the other emphasizes different aspects, multiplication
can selectively boost the importance of features that are deemed relevant by both streams
as shown in Fig. 7.


4.3 Training process

To train this network, stochastic gradient descent with momentum optimizers is employed.
The primary objective of this optimization method is to minimize the loss function by
iteratively updating the network parameters. This is achieved by taking small steps in the
direction of the negative gradient of the loss function, helping the network converge to a
better set of parameters over time. During the training process, mini-batches of size 28 are
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                              34295




Fig. 6  Activations from 3rd Layer




Fig. 7  Note this data is mandatory. Please provide



used, meaning that a subset of 28 training samples is processed together in each iteration.
This mini-batch approach helps to stabilize training and provides computational efficiency.
   The training process begins with an initial learning rate of 0.001, which dictates the
size of the steps taken during gradient descent. A momentum value of 0.9 is applied to
further aid convergence. This momentum term allows the optimizer to continue moving
34296                                        Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


in the direction of previously determined gradients, which can help smooth the conver-
gence path and escape local minima during training.

4.4 Performance measure of cattle muzzle print recognition

The performance measure for cattle muzzle print recognition serves as a critical evaluation
of the effectiveness of a system or model designed to identify and authenticate cattle based
on the unique patterns present in their muzzle prints.

Accuracy Accuracy measures the proportion of correctly recognized cattle based on their
muzzle prints. It is a fundamental indicator of the system’s overall performance.
                                               TP + TN
                             Accuracy =                                                         (2)
                                          FN + TN + TP + FP
   TP (True Positives) represents the number of correctly predicted positive instances, TN
(True Negatives) represents the number of correctly predicted negative instances. FP (False
Positives) represents the number of negative instances that were incorrectly predicted as
positive. FN (False Negatives) represents the number of positive instances that were incor-
rectly predicted as negative.

Precision and Recall Precision evaluates the proportion of correctly identified cattle
among those identified by the system, focusing on minimizing false positives. Recall, on
the other hand, assesses the proportion of correctly identified cattle among all the cattle
that should have been identified, emphasizing minimizing false negatives.
                                                   TP
                                   Precision =                                                  (3)
                                                 TP + FP

                                                  TP
                                     Recall =                                                   (4)
                                                TP + FN
F1 Score The F1 score combines precision and recall into a single metric, providing a bal-
anced measure of recognition performance. It is particularly useful when there is an imbal-
ance between positive and negative cases.
                                                 2TP
                                F1score =                                                       (5)
                                            2TP + FN + FP
The Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) MCC is a useful metric because it consid-
ers both sensitivity (recall) and specificity while being robust to imbalanced datasets. It is
often used in situations where the class distribution is unequal, and the consequences of
false positives and false negatives are different.
                                  TN × TP − FP × FN
                   MCC = √                                                                      (6)
                          (TN + FN)(FP + TP)(TN + FP)(FN + TP)

   In the context of cattle muzzle print recognition, the presented table of performance
measures serves as a comprehensive analytical tool for evaluating the efficacy of the sys-
tem under various configurations. These configurations encompass differing numbers of
layers and distinct filter types, specifically learnable filters and Gabor Filters.
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                              34297


4.4.1 Configuration 1: Two Streams with one block (Conv_1 / Conv_G_1 + Batch
       Normalization + ReLu) at each stream

In Configuration 1, the model employs two parallel streams, each containing a single
block composed of a convolution layer (Conv_1 for both streams), batch normalization,
and a ReLU activation function. The convolution layer applies a set of learnable fil-
ters to the input data, extracting essential features, while the batch normalization layer
normalizes the outputs to speed up training and provide regularization. This configura-
tion achieves an accuracy of 97.46% and a Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of
94.94%, indicating a strong balance between true positives and true negatives. Despite
its simplicity, this single-block-per-stream architecture demonstrates robust perfor-
mance and serves as a solid foundation for further experimentation and enhancement in
cattle muzzle print recognition.
    Transitioning to a one-layer architecture with one stream using learnable filters in
Conv_1 and the other stream using Gabor filters (Conv_G_1), a marginal reduction in
accuracy to 95.23% is observed. However, this configuration excels in specificity, reg-
istering an impressive value of 99.98%. This suggests a heightened ability to correctly
identify negative instances while maintaining an acceptable level of sensitivity. Due
to the absence of learnable parameters in a single-layer architecture employing Gabor
filters, the model’s accuracy experiences a decrease of approximately 2.23 percentage
points, shifting from 97.46% to 95.23%.


4.4.2 Configuration 2: Two Streams with Two blocks (Conv_1 / Conv_G_1 + Batch
       Normalization + ReLu) at each stream

With a progression to two layers and Learnable filters, the system demonstrates an ele-
vated accuracy of 97.77%. Here, precision emerges as a noteworthy metric, reaching
96.84%, underscoring the system’s precision in correctly classifying positive instances.
The pinnacle of performance manifests in the two-layer setup with Gabor Filters, where
an accuracy of 98.38% is attained. This configuration excels in recall, achieving 97.39%,
signifying its adeptness in recognizing positive instances. Furthermore, the precision
of 97.58% underscores its precision in correctly classifying positive instances. It is dis-
cernible that the selection of layers and filter types profoundly impacts the system’s per-
formance characteristics, offering flexibility and adaptability in tailoring the system to
meet specific recognition requirements in the domain of cattle muzzle print recognition.


4.4.3 Configuration 3: Two Streams with Three blocks (Conv_1 / Conv_G_1 + Batch
       Normalization + ReLu) at each stream

As we advance to three layer architecture with learnable filters, the system’s accu-
racy notably improves to 98.17%. The highest level of performance is observed in the
three -layer setup featuring Gabor Filters, where an impressive accuracy of 98.58% is
achieved. Upon transitioning to a four-layer architecture, we observe a slight decline in
performance metrics. However, when employing Gabor filters, the performance metrics
remain consistent with those achieved in the three-layer configuration. Consequently,
we conclude that a three-layer architecture is the preferred choice for our further analy-
sis, as it provides a balance between performance and complexity Table 1.
34298                                             Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


Table 1  Performance Measure of Muzzle Print Recognition by varying number of Blocks
Performance Measures         Configuration 1: Configuration 2: Configuration 3: Configuration 4:
                             Two Streams with Two Streams with Two Streams with Two Streams with
                             one block        Two blocks       Three blocks     Four blocks
                             *Case a *Case b *Case a *Case b *Case a *Case b *Case a *Case b

Accuracy                     0.9746    0.9523   0.9777     0.9838    0.9817   0.9858   0.9797    0.9858
Error                        0.0254    0.0477   0.0223     0.0162    0.0183   0.0142   0.0203    0.0142
Recall                       0.9528    0.9305   0.9667     0.9739    0.9707   0.9809   0.9697    0.9809
Specificity                  0.9999    0.9998   0.9999     0.9999    0.9999   0.9999   0.9999    0.9999
Precision                    0.9490    0.9319   0.9684     0.9758    0.9693   0.9837   0.9671    0.9837
False Positive Rate          0.0001    0.0002   0.0001     0.0001    0.0001   0.0001   0.0001    0.0001
F1_score                     0.9481    0.9240   0.9639     0.9727    0.9673   0.9796   0.9662    0.9796
Matthews Correlation         0.9494    0.9273   0.9656     0.9737    0.9686   0.9809   0.9672    0.9809
  Coefficient

*Case a: Learnable filters in both Streams
*Case b: Learnable filters in One Stream (Conv_1) and Gabor Filter (Conv_G_1) in other Stream



Table 2  Performance Measure of Muzzle Print Recognition by Two Stream Feature Fusion
Performance Measures                  Feature Map Fusion    Feature Map Fusion     Resnet like architec-
                                      by Multiplication     by Concatenation       ture with Gabor filters

Accuracy                              0.9899                0.9817                 0.9858
Error                                 0.0101                0.0183                 0.0142
Recall                                0.9836                0.9716                 0.9758
Specificity                           1.0000                0.9999                 0.9999
Precision                             0.9839                0.9759                 0.9801
FalsePositiveRate                     0.0000                0.0001                 0.0001
F1_score                              0.9818                0.9706                 0.9739
Matthews Correlation Coefficient      0.9827                0.9721                 0.9758



   The performance metrics for two distinct methods of feature map fusion, namely
Feature Map Fusion by Multiplication and Feature Map Fusion by Concatenation, are
presented in the Table 2. Notably, the multiplication approach yields a higher accuracy
rate of 98.99%, demonstrating its superior classification capabilities compared to the
concatenation approach, which achieves a slightly lower accuracy rate of 98.17%. In
terms of error rates, the multiplication method boasts a lower error rate of 1.01%, while
the concatenation approach exhibits a somewhat higher error rate of 1.83%.
   The architectural configuration comprises a single convolutional layer with Gabor fil-
ters, batch normalization, ReLU activation, and three subsequent max-pooling layers
within one stream. Simultaneously, the other stream incorporates three sets of convolu-
tional layers, each followed by batch normalization, ReLU activation, and max-pooling.
The fusion of feature maps through multiplication in this architecture yields an impressive
accuracy rate of 98.58%. This outcome underscores the substantial improvement in learn-
ing achieved through the incorporation of Gabor filters within the network. It highlights the
significance of Gabor filters as a valuable tool for enhancing the model’s ability to capture
Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301                                         34299


Table 3  Comparison of Performance Measure with State of the Art Work for Muzzle Print Recognition
Model                          Accuracy (%)               Model                         Accuracy (%)

AlexNet [38]                   96.5                       ResNet101 [38]                94.2
DenseNet201 [38]               94.6                       SqueezeNet_1.1 [38]           95.9
EfficientNet_b7 [38]           54.1                       VGG19_BN [38]                 98.1
GoogleNet [38]                 59.4                       Wide_ResNet50_2 [38]          89.6
InceptionV3 [38]               81.7                       Wide_ResNet101_2 [38]         90.4
Xception [38]                  58.3                       MobileNetV3_Large [38]        95.9
Proposed Method                98.99




intricate patterns and features, ultimately contributing to superior recognition performance
in the context of cattle muzzle print recognition.
    The Table 3 presents a comparative snapshot of the performance of various deep learn-
ing models in terms of accuracy (%). Each row corresponds to a different deep learning
architecture, and the associated accuracy score (%) reflects the model’s proficiency in that
task. Notable highlights include the high accuracy achieved by VGG19_BN at 98.1%,
exemplifying its effectiveness in image classification. In contrast, EfficientNet_b7 achieved
a relatively lower accuracy of 54.1. Meanwhile, SqueezeNet_1.1 and MobileNetV3_Large
demonstrated competitive accuracy levels at 95.9%, underlining their suitability for
resource-constrained environments. Wide_ResNet50_2 and Wide_ResNet101_2 show-
cased respectable accuracies at 89.6% and 90.4%, respectively, owing to their wider archi-
tecture. InceptionV3 demonstrated a balanced performance with an accuracy of 81.7%.
These accuracy scores serve as valuable benchmarks for selecting the most appropriate
model architecture based on the specific requirements of a given task, whether prioritizing
accuracy, efficiency, or a balance between the two.


5 Conclusion

In conclusion, this study addresses the critical domain of muzzle print recognition, a piv-
otal facet of biometric identification for animals, which has garnered considerable atten-
tion in recent times. Our innovative approach introduces a Two-Stream Convolutional Neu-
ral Network (CNN) integrated with Gabor Filters to significantly enhance precision and
robustness in muzzle print identification. The Two-Stream CNN architecture, consisting
of the Spatial Stream and the Texture Stream, synergizes spatial and texture information,
harnessing the collective strengths of both modalities. Leveraging Gabor Filters within
the Texture Stream enables the extraction of intricate texture details, rendering the model
highly sensitive to even subtle variations in muzzle patterns. Through meticulous training
and fine-tuning on a comprehensive dataset, specifically the Beef Cattle Muzzle/Nose print
database for individual identification, we achieved an exceptional recognition accuracy of
98.99%. The model’s capacity to accurately discern subtle nuances in muzzle prints holds
promise across a spectrum of applications, including wildlife preservation, animal moni-
toring, and heightened security measures, ushering in a new era of biometric identification
for animals.
34300                                                  Multimedia Tools and Applications (2025) 84:34285–34301


Data availability Data available on request from the authors.

Declarations
Conflict of interest The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.
